seoTitle: How to Filter Airtable and Remove Duplicates
metaDescription: Combine Airtable's filtering tools with the Dedupe extension to isolate and remove duplicate records from a targeted subset of data.
intro: Pairing Airtable's view filters with the Dedupe extension lets you narrow down to a specific subset of records before hunting for duplicates, which is faster and more accurate than scanning an entire large table at once. This guide covers setting up the filter, running Dedupe against the filtered set, and cleaning up the redundant rows.

steps.heading: Open your target table
steps.body: Navigate to the table containing the data you suspect has duplicates.

steps.heading: Add conditional filters
steps.body: Use the main view toolbar to add filtering rules that isolate the specific subset of records you're concerned about.

steps.heading: Configure value matches
steps.body: Set up specific value matches within your filter to further narrow down potential duplicate records.

steps.heading: Open the Dedupe extension
steps.body: Navigate to the Extensions menu and access the Dedupe cleanup tool.

steps.heading: Scan the filtered records
steps.body: Run the scan to identify and flag redundant row records within your filtered view.

steps.heading: Merge or delete
steps.body: Choose to merge conflicting data or permanently delete the target records, then execute the cleanup.

faq.question: Why filter before running the Dedupe scan?
faq.answer: Filtering narrows the scan to a relevant subset, which is faster and reduces false matches compared to scanning your whole table.

faq.question: Can I save a filtered view to reuse for future duplicate checks?
faq.answer: Yes, saving the filtered view lets you re-run this cleanup process periodically without rebuilding the filter each time.

faq.question: Will filtering hide any records permanently?
faq.answer: No, filters only change what's currently displayed — the underlying records remain in the table regardless of the active filter.

faq.question: Does this process work the same as running Dedupe on the whole table?
faq.answer: Yes, the same merge or delete steps apply, just scoped to the filtered subset instead of every record in the table.
